lessons.title: The Cell: Basic Unit of Life
objectives: Explain why the cell is considered the fundamental unit of life
Distinguish between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells
Identify the three main regions of a generalized human (eukaryotic) cell
Describe the general function of the plasma membrane

conceptBlocks.heading: Why Cells Matter in Healthcare
conceptBlocks.body: All body processes — from breathing to healing wounds — occur at the cellular level. Understanding cell biology is the foundation for understanding tissue health, disease mechanisms, and how medications work. Cancer is uncontrolled cell division. Infections disrupt normal cellular function. IV fluids affect fluid balance across cell membranes. Cell biology is not abstract — it is the molecular basis of every clinical decision.

conceptBlocks.heading: Prokaryotes vs Eukaryotes
conceptBlocks.body:
**Prokaryotic cells** — lack a membrane-bound nucleus; DNA floats freely in the cytoplasm. Bacteria are prokaryotes. This structural difference is the basis for why antibiotics can target bacterial cells without destroying human cells.

**Eukaryotic cells** — contain a membrane-bound nucleus housing DNA. All human, animal, plant, and fungal cells are eukaryotic. Human cells have highly organized internal compartments (organelles).

conceptBlocks.heading: The Three Regions of a Human Cell
conceptBlocks.body:
1. **Plasma membrane (cell membrane)** — the outer boundary; controls what enters and exits the cell

2. **Cytoplasm** — the fluid-filled interior containing organelles; mostly water with dissolved proteins, ions, and nutrients

3. **Nucleus** — the control center; contains DNA (the genetic blueprint) and directs protein synthesis and cell reproduction

conceptBlocks.heading: The Plasma Membrane
conceptBlocks.body:
The plasma membrane is a **phospholipid bilayer** — two layers of phospholipid molecules with their water-repelling (hydrophobic) tails sandwiched between their water-attracting (hydrophilic) heads.

Embedded in the bilayer:
• Protein channels — allow specific molecules to cross
• Receptor proteins — receive signals from hormones, drugs, and neurotransmitters
• Recognition proteins — identify the cell as 'self' (immune function)

This selective permeability allows the cell to maintain a controlled internal environment despite changes in the external environment.

lessons.teachingNotes: Start with the 'why': emphasize that understanding cells provides the molecular explanation for clinical concepts students will use every day. Use the antibiotic example (prokaryotes vs eukaryotes) to make the prokaryote/eukaryote distinction immediately clinically relevant.

lessons.title: The Nucleus and Genetic Control
objectives: Describe the structure and function of the nucleus and nuclear envelope
Explain the roles of DNA, RNA, and ribosomes in protein synthesis
Define the terms chromatin, chromosome, and gene
Explain why mutations in DNA can lead to disease

conceptBlocks.heading: Nuclear Structure
conceptBlocks.body:
The **nucleus** is enclosed in a **nuclear envelope** (double membrane) with **nuclear pores** that regulate the passage of molecules between the nucleus and cytoplasm.

Inside the nucleus:
• **Chromatin** — DNA wound around proteins (histones); loosely arranged when the cell is not dividing
• **Chromosomes** — condensed, visible chromatin structures that appear just before and during cell division; humans have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs)
• **Nucleolus** — region within the nucleus where ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is produced

conceptBlocks.heading: DNA: The Blueprint
conceptBlocks.body:
DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a double-stranded helix containing sequences of four bases: adenine (A), thymine (T), guanine (G), and cytosine (C). A **gene** is a specific sequence of DNA that codes for a protein.

**Central dogma of molecular biology:**
DNA → (transcription) → mRNA → (translation) → Protein

• Transcription: DNA template is copied into messenger RNA (mRNA) in the nucleus
• Translation: mRNA leaves the nucleus and is read by ribosomes to build proteins

conceptBlocks.heading: Ribosomes: Protein Factories
conceptBlocks.body:
**Ribosomes** are small organelles (not membrane-bound) composed of rRNA and proteins. They are the sites of protein synthesis (translation).

• **Free ribosomes** — float in the cytoplasm; make proteins for use inside the cell
• **Bound ribosomes** — attached to the rough endoplasmic reticulum; make proteins for export, membrane insertion, or organelle use

Proteins made by ribosomes include enzymes, structural proteins, hormones (e.g., insulin), and transport proteins (e.g., hemoglobin).

conceptBlocks.heading: Mutations and Disease
conceptBlocks.body:
A **mutation** is a change in the DNA base sequence. Mutations can:
• Be inherited (genetic disorders — e.g., sickle cell disease results from a single base pair change in the hemoglobin gene)
• Be acquired during a lifetime from radiation, carcinogens, or DNA replication errors

If a mutation occurs in a gene controlling cell division, it can lead to uncontrolled growth — **cancer**. Understanding mutations explains the basis of genetic testing and targeted cancer therapies.

lessons.teachingNotes: Use sickle cell disease as the clinical anchor for gene–protein–disease logic: one base pair mutation → wrong amino acid in hemoglobin → rigid sickle-shaped RBCs → vascular occlusion and pain crises. This chain connects molecular biology directly to a condition students will care for.

lessons.title: Cytoplasmic Organelles and Their Functions
objectives: Identify the major cytoplasmic organelles and describe the function of each
Explain the functional relationship between the rough ER, Golgi apparatus, and secretory vesicles
Describe how mitochondria produce ATP and why this matters clinically
Explain the roles of lysosomes and peroxisomes in cellular housekeeping

conceptBlocks.heading: Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER)
conceptBlocks.body:
**Rough ER** — studded with ribosomes on its outer surface; synthesizes and transports proteins destined for secretion or membrane insertion. Named 'rough' because of the ribosomes.

**Smooth ER** — no ribosomes; functions:
• Lipid and steroid hormone synthesis (e.g., cortisol, estrogen, testosterone)
• Drug detoxification (liver smooth ER)
• Calcium ion storage (especially in muscle cells — critical for muscle contraction)

conceptBlocks.heading: Golgi Apparatus: The Shipping Center
conceptBlocks.body:
The **Golgi apparatus** (Golgi complex) is a stack of flattened membrane sacs. It:
• Receives proteins from the rough ER
• Modifies, packages, and labels proteins (adding sugar chains = glycosylation)
• Ships proteins in **vesicles** to their final destinations:
  - Secretory vesicles → exocytosis to outside the cell (e.g., insulin release)
  - Lysosomes → intracellular digestion
  - Plasma membrane → new membrane proteins

conceptBlocks.heading: Mitochondria: The Energy Producers
conceptBlocks.body:
Mitochondria are double-membrane organelles responsible for producing **ATP** (adenosine triphosphate) — the cell's primary energy currency — through **cellular respiration**.

Key steps: glucose is broken down via glycolysis → Krebs cycle → electron transport chain → ATP

**Clinical relevance:**
• Cells with high energy demands (cardiac muscle, neurons) have many mitochondria
• Hypoxia (low oxygen) impairs ATP production → cell injury and death (MI, stroke)
• Medications like metformin affect mitochondrial energy pathways

conceptBlocks.heading: Lysosomes and Peroxisomes
conceptBlocks.body:
**Lysosomes** — membrane-bound sacs containing powerful digestive enzymes; break down:
• Worn-out organelles (autophagy)
• Bacteria engulfed by phagocytosis
• Cellular debris

**Lysosomal storage diseases** (e.g., Tay-Sachs, Gaucher) occur when a lysosomal enzyme is missing — undigested material accumulates, damaging cells.

**Peroxisomes** — break down fatty acids and neutralize harmful chemicals (like hydrogen peroxide using catalase). Important in liver and kidney detoxification.

lessons.teachingNotes: The rough ER → Golgi → secretory vesicle pathway is the 'protein highway.' Use insulin as the example: produced by ribosomes on rough ER, modified in Golgi, packaged in secretory vesicles, released (exocytosis) when blood glucose rises. This connects organelle biology directly to diabetes and insulin therapy.

lessons.title: Cell Specialization and Homeostasis
objectives: Explain how cell specialization relates to tissue and organ function
Define homeostasis and describe how cells contribute to maintaining it
Describe the consequences of homeostatic imbalance at the cellular level
Identify the relationship between cellular health and the clinical concept of organ failure

conceptBlocks.heading: Cell Specialization
conceptBlocks.body:
All human cells arise from a single fertilized egg yet develop into over 200 distinct cell types — each specialized to perform specific functions:

• Muscle cells → contraction (abundant mitochondria, actin/myosin proteins)
• Neurons → electrical signaling (long axons, myelin for fast conduction)
• Erythrocytes (RBCs) → oxygen transport (packed with hemoglobin; no nucleus in mature RBCs)
• Goblet cells → mucus secretion (abundant Golgi, secretory vesicles)
• Osteoblasts → bone matrix production (active rough ER)

Specialization is driven by gene expression — all cells have the same DNA, but different genes are turned on or off in different cell types.

conceptBlocks.heading: Homeostasis: Cellular Level
conceptBlocks.body:
Homeostasis = the body's ability to maintain a stable internal environment despite changing conditions.

At the cellular level:
• pH is maintained near 7.4 (enzymes function within narrow pH range)
• Temperature is regulated (enzyme denaturation occurs above ~40°C)
• Ion concentrations are controlled (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺ determine membrane excitability)
• Oxygen and glucose are continuously supplied to support ATP production

Feedback mechanisms (primarily negative feedback) detect deviations and initiate corrective responses.

conceptBlocks.heading: Cellular Injury and Death
conceptBlocks.body:
When cells cannot maintain homeostasis, injury occurs:

**Reversible injury** — cell swelling, ion pump failure (if restored quickly, cell recovers)
**Irreversible injury** — membrane rupture, ATP depletion, calcium influx → cell death

Two types of cell death:
• **Apoptosis** — programmed, orderly cell death (normal development, immune cell clearance)
• **Necrosis** — chaotic, inflammatory cell death from injury (trauma, ischemia, toxins)

**Clinical connection:** Myocardial infarction → ischemia → ATP depletion → irreversible cardiomyocyte death → necrosis → elevated cardiac enzymes (troponin) in blood

lessons.teachingNotes: Close the loop on the clinical relevance of cell biology by tracing MI from blocked coronary artery → oxygen deprivation → mitochondria stop producing ATP → ion pumps fail → irreversible cell injury → necrosis → troponin release. This is the exact mechanism behind why troponin elevation = cardiac cell death.

questions.stem: The fundamental unit of all living organisms is the:

options.A: Tissue
options.B: Organ
options.C: Cell
options.D: Organelle

questions.rationale: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms. Tissues are formed by groups of similar cells. Organs are structures made of multiple tissue types. Organelles are subcellular structures within cells.
questions.keyConceptTested: cell as basic unit of life

questions.stem: Antibiotics can target bacterial cells without destroying human cells primarily because:

options.A: Human cells have a plasma membrane that bacterial cells lack
options.B: Bacterial cells are prokaryotic and lack a membrane-bound nucleus, unlike human eukaryotic cells
options.C: Human cells are larger and can expel antibiotics before they cause harm
options.D: Antibiotics only affect cells that produce cell walls made of chitin

questions.rationale: Bacteria are prokaryotes with distinct structural features (no membrane-bound nucleus, different ribosomes, cell walls containing peptidoglycan) that antibiotics specifically target. Human cells are eukaryotes with fundamentally different structures, so most antibiotics spare them. This is the basis of antibiotic selectivity.
questions.keyConceptTested: prokaryote vs eukaryote — antibiotic relevance

questions.stem: The plasma membrane is best described as:

options.A: A rigid cell wall that provides structural support
options.B: A selectively permeable phospholipid bilayer that controls substance entry and exit
options.C: A solid barrier that prevents all molecules from crossing
options.D: A protein-only layer that surrounds the nucleus

questions.rationale: The plasma membrane is a selectively permeable phospholipid bilayer. It regulates what enters and exits the cell through embedded protein channels, receptors, and carrier molecules. Rigid cell walls are found in plants and bacteria, not human cells. The plasma membrane surrounds the entire cell, not just the nucleus (that is the nuclear envelope).
questions.keyConceptTested: plasma membrane — phospholipid bilayer; selective permeability

questions.stem: Which organelle is responsible for producing ATP through cellular respiration?

options.A: Ribosome
options.B: Golgi apparatus
options.C: Mitochondria
options.D: Lysosome

questions.rationale: Mitochondria are the primary sites of ATP (adenosine triphosphate) production via cellular respiration, using oxygen and glucose. They are often called the 'powerhouse of the cell.' Ribosomes = protein synthesis. Golgi apparatus = modifies and packages proteins. Lysosomes = intracellular digestion.
questions.keyConceptTested: mitochondria — ATP production

questions.stem: In protein synthesis, the correct order of steps is:

options.A: Translation → transcription → protein
options.B: DNA → transcription → mRNA → translation → protein
options.C: RNA → nucleus → DNA → ribosome → protein
options.D: Ribosome → Golgi → DNA → protein

questions.rationale: The central dogma: DNA is transcribed into mRNA in the nucleus → mRNA travels to ribosomes → ribosomes translate mRNA into protein. This one-directional flow (DNA → RNA → Protein) is fundamental to all cell biology. Option A reverses transcription and translation.
questions.keyConceptTested: central dogma: DNA → RNA → Protein

questions.stem: Lysosomes function primarily to:

options.A: Produce energy for the cell
options.B: Synthesize lipids and steroid hormones
options.C: Package and ship proteins to their destinations
options.D: Digest worn-out organelles, bacteria, and cellular debris

questions.rationale: Lysosomes contain powerful digestive enzymes that break down cellular debris, worn-out organelles (autophagy), and engulfed bacteria (after phagocytosis). They are the cell's 'recycling and waste disposal' system. Mitochondria = energy. Smooth ER = lipid/steroid synthesis. Golgi apparatus = packages and ships proteins.
questions.keyConceptTested: lysosomes — intracellular digestion

questions.stem: The Golgi apparatus is best described as the cell's:

options.A: Energy producer
options.B: Genetic library
options.C: Protein modification and shipping center
options.D: Site of DNA replication

questions.rationale: The Golgi apparatus receives proteins from the rough ER, modifies them (e.g., adds sugar chains), then packages and ships them to their final destinations (secretion, plasma membrane, or lysosomes). It is the cell's distribution and post-processing center. Mitochondria = energy. Nucleus = genetic library and site of DNA replication.
questions.keyConceptTested: Golgi apparatus function

questions.stem: A patient with myocardial ischemia (insufficient blood supply to the heart muscle) is at risk for cell death because:

options.A: Ischemia causes the nucleus to stop producing DNA
options.B: Without oxygen, mitochondria cannot produce adequate ATP, leading to cellular energy failure
options.C: Ischemia destroys the Golgi apparatus, stopping protein distribution
options.D: Reduced blood flow causes the plasma membrane to become permanently rigid

questions.rationale: Ischemia = inadequate oxygen delivery. Without oxygen, mitochondria cannot complete cellular respiration, so ATP production drops rapidly. ATP-dependent ion pumps (Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase) fail → cell swelling → membrane disruption → irreversible cell death (necrosis). This is the cellular mechanism underlying myocardial infarction.
questions.keyConceptTested: ischemia → ATP depletion → cell death (clinical application)

questions.stem: The smooth endoplasmic reticulum (smooth ER) is responsible for all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: Lipid and steroid hormone synthesis
options.B: Drug detoxification in liver cells
options.C: Calcium ion storage for muscle contraction
options.D: Protein synthesis from mRNA templates

questions.rationale: Smooth ER does NOT have ribosomes and does not perform protein synthesis — that is the function of rough ER and free ribosomes. Smooth ER functions include lipid/steroid synthesis, drug detoxification (liver), and calcium storage (especially in muscle cells for contraction). Protein synthesis requires ribosomes, which are absent from smooth ER.
questions.keyConceptTested: smooth ER vs rough ER functions

questions.stem: Which cell type would you expect to have the most mitochondria?

options.A: Adipocytes (fat storage cells)
options.B: Erythrocytes (red blood cells)
options.C: Cardiomyocytes (heart muscle cells)
options.D: Lymphocytes (immune cells at rest)

questions.rationale: Cardiomyocytes (heart muscle cells) contract continuously without rest, requiring massive amounts of ATP. Therefore they have a very high density of mitochondria (~30% of cell volume). Mature erythrocytes have NO mitochondria (they use glycolysis only and lack nuclei). Fat storage and resting immune cells have lower energy demands.
questions.keyConceptTested: mitochondria abundance correlates with energy demand

questions.stem: The nuclear envelope is best described as:

options.A: A single membrane surrounding the entire cell
options.B: A double membrane containing pores that surrounds the nucleus
options.C: The outer layer of the Golgi apparatus
options.D: A protein scaffold that holds the nucleus in place

questions.rationale: The nuclear envelope is a double membrane (two phospholipid bilayers) that surrounds the nucleus. It contains nuclear pores that regulate the passage of materials between the nucleus and cytoplasm (e.g., mRNA leaving; proteins entering). The plasma membrane = single bilayer surrounding the cell.
questions.keyConceptTested: nuclear envelope — double membrane with pores

questions.stem: The hormone insulin is produced in pancreatic beta cells. Which organelle pathway is insulin most likely to follow before secretion?

options.A: Smooth ER → lysosome → exocytosis
options.B: Rough ER → Golgi apparatus → secretory vesicle → exocytosis
options.C: Free ribosome → nucleus → plasma membrane
options.D: Mitochondria → Golgi → endocytosis

questions.rationale: Insulin is a secreted protein. Secreted proteins follow the pathway: synthesized on ribosomes of the rough ER → modified in the Golgi apparatus → packaged in secretory vesicles → released via exocytosis when blood glucose rises. Free ribosomes make proteins for internal use. Mitochondria produce energy, not proteins for secretion.
questions.keyConceptTested: protein secretion pathway: rough ER → Golgi → vesicle → exocytosis

questions.stem: The term 'apoptosis' refers to:

options.A: Chaotic, inflammatory cell death from injury
options.B: Programmed, orderly cell death that is a normal process
options.C: Uncontrolled cell division leading to tumor growth
options.D: The engulfment of dead cells by phagocytes

questions.rationale: Apoptosis = programmed cell death — an orderly, genetically controlled process that eliminates unwanted cells without inflammation (e.g., removal of web-like tissue between fingers during fetal development; elimination of immune cells after an infection resolves). Necrosis = chaotic cell death from injury. Hyperplasia/malignancy = uncontrolled division. Phagocytosis = engulfment by immune cells.
questions.keyConceptTested: apoptosis vs necrosis

questions.stem: What is a gene?

options.A: A complete set of 46 chromosomes in a human cell
options.B: A segment of DNA that codes for a specific protein
options.C: The condensed form of chromatin visible during cell division
options.D: A strand of mRNA produced after transcription

questions.rationale: A gene is a specific sequence of nucleotides on a DNA molecule that contains the instructions for making a specific protein. The human genome contains approximately 20,000–25,000 genes. A chromosome contains thousands of genes. Chromatin = loosely arranged DNA + proteins. mRNA = the RNA copy made from the gene during transcription.
questions.keyConceptTested: definition of a gene

questions.stem: A patient's lab results show elevated serum troponin levels. Using cellular biology knowledge, this finding indicates:

options.A: Lysosomes in the liver have released digestive enzymes
options.B: Cardiac muscle cell membranes have ruptured, releasing intracellular proteins into the blood
options.C: Mitochondria in the heart are producing excess ATP
options.D: Protein synthesis has increased in response to cardiac stress

questions.rationale: Troponin is an intracellular cardiac protein. When cardiomyocytes undergo irreversible injury (necrosis), their plasma membranes rupture and intracellular proteins — including troponin — are released into the bloodstream. Elevated serum troponin = cardiac muscle cell death (as in myocardial infarction). This is a critical cardiac biomarker.
questions.keyConceptTested: cell membrane rupture → intracellular protein release → troponin elevation

questions.stem: Homeostasis at the cellular level is best described as:

options.A: The cell undergoing division to replace damaged cells
options.B: The cell's ability to maintain a stable internal environment despite external changes
options.C: The cell's ability to move toward nutrient sources
options.D: The synthesis of new proteins in response to DNA mutations

questions.rationale: Cellular homeostasis = the maintenance of stable internal conditions (pH ~7.4, ion balance, temperature, oxygen supply, ATP levels) despite changes in the external environment. When homeostasis is disrupted and cannot be restored, cell injury and death occur. Division is cell reproduction, not homeostasis.
questions.keyConceptTested: cellular homeostasis definition

questions.stem: Mature red blood cells (erythrocytes) lack a nucleus. This means they:

options.A: Cannot produce any proteins and have a limited lifespan
options.B: Divide more rapidly than nucleated cells
options.C: Contain double the amount of DNA in the cytoplasm
options.D: Rely on the Golgi apparatus as their control center

questions.rationale: Without a nucleus, mature RBCs cannot make new proteins or reproduce. Their hemoglobin content is fixed at maturation. This limits their lifespan to approximately 120 days, after which they are destroyed and replaced by new RBCs from bone marrow. The Golgi apparatus does not substitute as a control center.
questions.keyConceptTested: erythrocyte lacks nucleus → limited lifespan; cell specialization

questions.stem: The nucleolus within the nucleus is responsible for:

options.A: Storing DNA between cell divisions
options.B: Producing ribosomal RNA (rRNA) used to build ribosomes
options.C: Packaging proteins for transport out of the nucleus
options.D: Breaking down damaged nuclear proteins

questions.rationale: The nucleolus is a distinct region within the nucleus where ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is produced and ribosome subunits are assembled. These are then exported through nuclear pores to the cytoplasm where they function in protein synthesis. The nucleolus is not involved in protein packaging (Golgi) or degradation (proteasome/lysosomes).
questions.keyConceptTested: nucleolus — rRNA production

questions.stem: A lysosomal storage disease occurs when a patient lacks a specific lysosomal enzyme. Which consequence would most likely result?

options.A: The cell produces excess ATP from undigested material
options.B: Undigested substrates accumulate inside lysosomes, damaging the cell
options.C: The Golgi apparatus compensates by releasing extra enzymes
options.D: The smooth ER absorbs the undigested material for lipid synthesis

questions.rationale: Lysosomes contain specific digestive enzymes. If an enzyme is absent (genetic deficiency), the substrate that enzyme would normally break down accumulates inside the lysosome. Over time, swollen lysosomes rupture or disrupt cell function. Examples: Tay-Sachs (GM2 ganglioside accumulation in neurons), Gaucher disease (glucocerebrosides in macrophages). No organelle can fully compensate.
questions.keyConceptTested: lysosomal storage disease mechanism

questions.stem: Which of the following best explains why sickle cell disease causes painful vascular occlusion crises?

options.A: A mutation in the hemoglobin gene produces an abnormal protein that causes RBCs to become rigid and sickle-shaped, obstructing small vessels
options.B: Sickle cells produce excess ATP that causes vessel constriction
options.C: Lysosomal enzymes in RBCs are absent, causing cell membrane rupture
options.D: Abnormal ribosomal function reduces hemoglobin production overall

questions.rationale: Sickle cell disease results from a single nucleotide mutation in the beta-globin gene → the amino acid valine replaces glutamic acid → abnormal hemoglobin S polymerizes under low oxygen → rigid sickle-shaped RBCs → vascular occlusion → pain crisis and organ damage. This directly demonstrates the gene → protein → disease pathway.
questions.keyConceptTested: gene mutation → abnormal protein → disease (sickle cell model)

questions.stem: The rough endoplasmic reticulum differs from the smooth ER in that it:

options.A: Contains more ATP than the smooth ER
options.B: Has ribosomes on its outer surface and is the site of secretory protein synthesis
options.C: Is found only in muscle cells
options.D: Produces lipids and detoxifies drugs

questions.rationale: The rough ER is studded with ribosomes on its outer surface, giving it a 'rough' appearance. These ribosomes synthesize proteins destined for secretion (like hormones, antibodies) or for membrane insertion. Smooth ER has no ribosomes and is involved in lipid synthesis and drug detoxification. Both types are found in most cells, though proportions vary.
questions.keyConceptTested: rough ER vs smooth ER — ribosomes

questions.stem: The plasma membrane contains receptor proteins. These receptors are clinically important because they:

options.A: Produce ATP for signal transduction
options.B: Serve as binding sites for hormones, drugs, and neurotransmitters to exert their effects on the cell
options.C: Protect the cell from osmotic pressure by blocking all water entry
options.D: Digest extracellular bacteria before they enter the cell

questions.rationale: Membrane receptor proteins are the binding sites (lock-and-key model) for hormones (e.g., insulin binds to insulin receptors), drugs (e.g., beta-blockers bind to beta-adrenergic receptors), and neurotransmitters (e.g., acetylcholine binds to cholinergic receptors). This is the molecular basis for pharmacology — drugs work by activating or blocking specific receptors.
questions.keyConceptTested: membrane receptors — basis of pharmacology

questions.stem: The cytoskeleton of a cell provides all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: Structural support and shape maintenance
options.B: Intracellular transport tracks for vesicles and organelles
options.C: Generation of ATP through cellular respiration
options.D: Enables cell movement and division via spindle fibers

questions.rationale: The cytoskeleton (microtubules, microfilaments, and intermediate filaments) provides structural support, intracellular transport tracks (microtubules for vesicles/organelles), and enables cell division (mitotic spindle) and movement (actin-based). ATP generation is the function of mitochondria via cellular respiration — the cytoskeleton does not produce energy.
questions.keyConceptTested: cytoskeleton functions

questions.stem: Microtubules are a component of the cytoskeleton. They are critical for cell division because they:

options.A: Produce ATP needed to pull chromosomes apart
options.B: Form the mitotic spindle that physically separates chromosomes during cell division
options.C: Replicate DNA in the nucleus during S phase
options.D: Provide the structural backbone of the nuclear envelope

questions.rationale: Microtubules form the mitotic spindle — a structure of dynamic protein polymers that attaches to chromosomes at kinetochores and physically pulls sister chromatids to opposite poles of the cell during anaphase. Taxane drugs (e.g., paclitaxel) stabilize microtubules abnormally, preventing spindle disassembly and blocking cell division.
questions.keyConceptTested: microtubules — mitotic spindle; taxane mechanism

questions.stem: Cilia on the surface of respiratory epithelial cells differ from the flagellum of a sperm cell in that:

options.A: Cilia use ATP while flagella do not require energy
options.B: Cilia are numerous and short, beating rhythmically; flagella are single and long, propelling the cell
options.C: Cilia propel the cell; flagella move substances across the cell surface
options.D: Cilia are found only in non-dividing cells; flagella in dividing cells

questions.rationale: Cilia are multiple, short, hair-like projections that beat in coordinated waves to move substances across a cell surface (e.g., mucus in airways). Flagella are typically single, long whip-like structures that propel the cell itself (e.g., sperm motility). Both use ATP through dynein motor proteins and have the same 9+2 microtubule arrangement.
questions.keyConceptTested: cilia vs flagella — structure and function

questions.stem: Peroxisomes in the liver are important for drug detoxification because they:

options.A: Package detoxified drugs in vesicles for exocytosis
options.B: Break down harmful hydrogen peroxide and oxidize toxic substances using catalase and oxidases
options.C: Synthesize the enzymes used by lysosomes to break down cellular waste
options.D: Transport drugs across the plasma membrane using ATP-driven pumps

questions.rationale: Peroxisomes contain oxidative enzymes (oxidases) that generate hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) while breaking down fatty acids and detoxifying harmful substances. Catalase within the peroxisome immediately breaks down H₂O₂ into water and O₂, preventing oxidative damage. This is especially active in liver cells (hepatocytes) that process drugs and alcohol.
questions.keyConceptTested: peroxisomes — oxidative detoxification; catalase

questions.stem: In muscle cells, the smooth ER is called the sarcoplasmic reticulum. Its specialized function is to:

options.A: Produce the myosin proteins used in muscle contraction
options.B: Store and release calcium ions that trigger muscle contraction
options.C: Generate ATP for the energy-intensive process of contraction
options.D: Remove waste products of contraction from the cytoplasm

questions.rationale: The sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) is the specialized smooth ER of muscle cells. Its primary function is to store and release Ca²⁺ ions. A nerve signal triggers Ca²⁺ release from the SR → Ca²⁺ binds troponin → actin-myosin crossbridges form → contraction. After contraction, Ca²⁺ is pumped back into the SR. Calcium channel blockers can affect this process.
questions.keyConceptTested: sarcoplasmic reticulum — Ca²⁺ storage for muscle contraction

questions.stem: Cholesterol is an important component of the plasma membrane because it:

options.A: Acts as a receptor for hormones and drugs
options.B: Provides the phospholipid bilayer's hydrophilic outer surface
options.C: Stabilizes membrane fluidity, preventing it from becoming too rigid in cold or too fluid in heat
options.D: Generates ATP to maintain the membrane potential

questions.rationale: Cholesterol molecules are interspersed between phospholipids in the bilayer. They act as fluidity buffers: at low temperatures, cholesterol prevents phospholipids from packing too tightly (preventing rigidity); at high temperatures, cholesterol dampens excessive membrane fluidity. This is why membrane function is temperature-sensitive and why cells maintain precise internal temperature.
questions.keyConceptTested: cholesterol — membrane fluidity buffer

questions.stem: The glycocalyx is a carbohydrate-rich coat on the outer surface of the plasma membrane. It functions primarily in:

options.A: Energy production through glycolysis
options.B: Cell recognition, cell-to-cell communication, and immune identity
options.C: Protein synthesis on the outer membrane surface
options.D: Transporting lipids from the cell to the bloodstream

questions.rationale: The glycocalyx is formed by carbohydrates linked to membrane proteins (glycoproteins) and lipids (glycolipids). It serves as a molecular 'name tag' for cell recognition — enabling immune cells to identify 'self' vs 'foreign,' facilitating cell-to-cell adhesion, and allowing receptor-ligand binding. ABO blood typing is based on glycocalyx antigens on RBCs.
questions.keyConceptTested: glycocalyx — cell recognition and identity; ABO blood types

questions.stem: The proteasome is a cellular structure responsible for:

options.A: Synthesizing proteins from mRNA templates
options.B: Breaking down misfolded or damaged proteins marked with ubiquitin
options.C: Transporting newly made proteins from the rough ER to the Golgi
options.D: Storing proteins until they are needed for secretion

questions.rationale: The proteasome is a large protein complex that degrades ubiquitin-tagged proteins — typically misfolded, damaged, or regulatory proteins that must be removed. This 'quality control' system prevents accumulation of defective proteins. Proteasome dysfunction is implicated in neurodegenerative diseases (Parkinson's, Alzheimer's). Proteasome inhibitors (e.g., bortezomib) are used in multiple myeloma.
questions.keyConceptTested: proteasome — ubiquitin-proteasome protein degradation

questions.stem: Autophagy is a cellular process in which:

options.A: The cell generates energy by oxidizing fatty acids in peroxisomes
options.B: The cell engulfs and destroys its own damaged organelles by routing them to lysosomes
options.C: A cell self-destructs in a programmed, orderly manner (apoptosis)
options.D: Cells divide to replace neighboring cells that have died

questions.rationale: Auto- = self; -phagy = eating. Autophagy = 'self-eating' — a process in which the cell wraps damaged organelles in an autophagosome membrane and delivers them to lysosomes for enzymatic degradation and recycling. This is essential for cellular housekeeping, particularly during starvation (recycling nutrients) and in clearing damaged organelles. Defective autophagy is linked to neurodegeneration.
questions.keyConceptTested: autophagy — self-digestion of organelles via lysosomes

questions.stem: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are produced as a byproduct of mitochondrial respiration. They are clinically relevant because they:

options.A: Provide additional ATP when oxygen supply is adequate
options.B: Can damage DNA, proteins, and membrane lipids, contributing to aging and cancer
options.C: Signal the nucleus to increase protein synthesis during exercise
options.D: Stimulate red blood cell production in bone marrow

questions.rationale: ROS (superoxide, hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radicals) are produced during mitochondrial electron transport. They are highly reactive and can oxidatively damage DNA, proteins, and lipid membranes — a process called oxidative stress. Antioxidants (vitamin C, E, glutathione) neutralize ROS. Excessive ROS damage contributes to aging, atherosclerosis, neurodegenerative disease, and cancer.
questions.keyConceptTested: reactive oxygen species — oxidative stress; clinical implications

questions.stem: Stem cells are clinically valuable because they are capable of:

options.A: Producing large amounts of ATP without requiring oxygen
options.B: Indefinite self-renewal and differentiation into multiple specialized cell types
options.C: Repairing DNA mutations in fully differentiated neighboring cells
options.D: Secreting growth factors that suppress cancer cell division

questions.rationale: Stem cells are defined by two key properties: (1) self-renewal — the ability to divide and produce more stem cells, and (2) differentiation potential — the ability to give rise to specialized cell types. This makes them candidates for regenerative therapies (bone marrow transplants are the established example). Pluripotent stem cells can form any cell type; multipotent stem cells are more restricted (e.g., hematopoietic stem cells produce only blood cells).
questions.keyConceptTested: stem cells — self-renewal and differentiation

questions.stem: Which cellular structure anchors the mitotic spindle and organizes microtubule assembly during cell division?

options.A: Nucleolus
options.B: Centrosome (containing centrioles)
options.C: Rough endoplasmic reticulum
options.D: Golgi apparatus

questions.rationale: The centrosome, which contains a pair of centrioles, is the main microtubule-organizing center of the cell. During mitosis, the centrosome duplicates and each moves to an opposite pole, organizing the mitotic spindle. Microtubules grow from the centrosomes to attach to chromosomes. The nucleolus makes rRNA. Rough ER synthesizes secretory proteins.
questions.keyConceptTested: centrosome — microtubule organizing center; mitotic spindle

questions.stem: During an ischemic event, cellular injury begins with failure of the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump. In which cellular region does this pump reside?

options.A: Nuclear envelope
options.B: Plasma membrane
options.C: Golgi apparatus membrane
options.D: Mitochondrial inner membrane

questions.rationale: The Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump is an integral protein embedded in the plasma membrane (cell membrane). It uses ATP to pump Na⁺ out of and K⁺ into the cell. When ischemia depletes ATP, this pump fails — Na⁺ accumulates intracellularly, water follows osmotically, and the cell swells. This is one of the earliest events in ischemic cell injury.
questions.keyConceptTested: Na⁺/K⁺ pump location — plasma membrane; ischemia sequence

questions.stem: The process by which a cell engulfs extracellular material by folding the plasma membrane around it to form an intracellular vesicle is called:

options.A: Exocytosis
options.B: Endocytosis
options.C: Autophagy
options.D: Apoptosis

questions.rationale: Endocytosis = the cell membrane folds inward around extracellular material, pinching off to form an intracellular vesicle (endosome). Forms include phagocytosis (large particles), pinocytosis (fluid), and receptor-mediated endocytosis (specific molecules). Exocytosis = vesicles fuse with membrane to release contents out. Autophagy = digestion of own organelles. Apoptosis = programmed cell death.
questions.keyConceptTested: endocytosis definition

questions.stem: Karyotyping is a laboratory technique that examines a cell's chromosomes. It is performed on cells during which phase of mitosis, when chromosomes are most condensed and visible?

options.A: Interphase (G1)
options.B: S phase
options.C: Metaphase
options.D: Telophase

questions.rationale: Chromosomes are maximally condensed and individually distinguishable during metaphase, when they are also aligned at the metaphase plate. Karyotyping arrests cells in metaphase (using colchicine, a microtubule inhibitor) to photograph and arrange chromosomes for analysis. This technique detects numerical abnormalities (trisomy 21) and structural rearrangements.
questions.keyConceptTested: karyotyping — metaphase; chromosomes condensed

questions.stem: A cell in the G0 phase of the cell cycle is:

options.A: Actively replicating its DNA
options.B: Preparing to enter mitosis
options.C: In a quiescent, non-dividing state
options.D: Undergoing apoptosis

questions.rationale: G0 (Gap zero) is a non-dividing, quiescent state. Cells exit the active cell cycle and enter G0 when they are terminally differentiated (neurons, cardiac muscle) or when growth signals are absent. G0 cells can re-enter the cycle if stimulated (e.g., wound repair). This is distinct from apoptosis (programmed death) or active cell cycle phases.
questions.keyConceptTested: G0 phase — quiescent non-dividing state

questions.stem: A research team develops a drug that blocks nuclear pores. What cellular process would be most directly impaired?

options.A: Lipid synthesis in the smooth ER
options.B: Transport of mRNA from the nucleus to the cytoplasm for translation
options.C: Protein packaging in the Golgi apparatus
options.D: ATP production in mitochondria

questions.rationale: Nuclear pores regulate the bidirectional transport of molecules between the nucleus and cytoplasm. Blocking them would prevent mRNA (transcribed in the nucleus) from exiting to ribosomes in the cytoplasm — halting protein synthesis. It would also block proteins needed by the nucleus (DNA polymerases, histones) from entering. Smooth ER, Golgi, and mitochondria operate outside the nuclear envelope.
questions.keyConceptTested: nuclear pores — mRNA export; protein import

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the concept of 'cell senescence'?

options.A: The process by which cells divide more rapidly with age
options.B: A state in which cells permanently stop dividing but remain metabolically active and secrete inflammatory factors
options.C: Programmed death of cells following completion of their functional lifespan
options.D: The loss of cell membrane integrity in aging tissues

questions.rationale: Cellular senescence is a stress response in which cells permanently arrest their cell cycle (stop dividing) but do not die. Senescent cells remain alive and secrete pro-inflammatory cytokines and proteases (the senescence-associated secretory phenotype, SASP). Accumulation of senescent cells contributes to aging and age-related diseases. This is distinct from apoptosis (cell death) or normal cell cycle arrest.
questions.keyConceptTested: cell senescence — permanent cell cycle arrest; aging

questions.stem: Microfilaments (actin filaments) in the cytoskeleton are responsible for:

options.A: Forming the mitotic spindle during cell division
options.B: Cell motility, shape changes, and muscle contraction at the molecular level
options.C: Providing a structural scaffold for the nuclear envelope
options.D: Transporting vesicles between the ER and Golgi apparatus

questions.rationale: Microfilaments are made of actin polymers and are responsible for cell motility (lamellipodia, filopodia), shape changes during cytokinesis (cleavage furrow), and are the basis of muscle contraction (actin-myosin interaction in sarcomeres). Microtubules form the mitotic spindle. Intermediate filaments reinforce the nuclear lamina. Microtubules also serve as vesicle transport tracks.
questions.keyConceptTested: microfilaments (actin) — cell motility and muscle contraction

questions.stem: Which of the following is a correct statement about DNA and chromosomes?

options.A: Each chromosome contains a single gene
options.B: Humans have 46 chromosomes arranged in 23 pairs in somatic (body) cells
options.C: Chromosomes are only visible as distinct structures during interphase
options.D: Each chromosome contains DNA from both parents simultaneously

questions.rationale: Human somatic (non-sex) cells contain 46 chromosomes in 23 homologous pairs (diploid). Each chromosome contains thousands of genes. Chromosomes become visible as distinct condensed structures during mitosis (M phase), not interphase (where they exist as diffuse chromatin). Each chromosome within a pair comes from one parent (one maternal, one paternal).
questions.keyConceptTested: human chromosome number — 46 chromosomes; 23 pairs

questions.stem: A patient with Wilson's disease accumulates toxic levels of copper in liver cells because the transport protein that normally exports copper is absent. This disease best illustrates the importance of:

options.A: Lysosomes for heavy metal storage
options.B: Plasma membrane transport proteins for regulating intracellular ion concentrations
options.C: Peroxisomes for oxidizing heavy metals
options.D: Golgi apparatus for tagging metals with sorting signals

questions.rationale: Wilson's disease is caused by a mutation in the ATP7B gene, which encodes a copper-transporting ATPase (an active transporter in the plasma membrane). Without this transporter, copper accumulates in liver, brain, and other organs causing toxicity. This directly illustrates the role of membrane transport proteins in maintaining safe intracellular ion concentrations — an application of active transport biology.
questions.keyConceptTested: membrane transport proteins — Wilson's disease; ion homeostasis

questions.stem: What distinguishes the two layers of the phospholipid bilayer in terms of their molecular orientation?

options.A: Hydrophilic phosphate heads face inward; hydrophobic fatty acid tails face outward toward the aqueous environments
options.B: Hydrophilic phosphate heads face outward toward aqueous environments; hydrophobic tails face each other inward
options.C: Both layers have hydrophobic heads on the outside and hydrophilic tails inside
options.D: The two layers are chemically identical and face the same direction

questions.rationale: Each phospholipid has a hydrophilic (water-loving) head (phosphate group) and two hydrophobic (water-fearing) fatty acid tails. In the bilayer, hydrophilic heads face outward toward the aqueous extracellular fluid and cytoplasm, while hydrophobic tails face inward away from water. This arrangement is thermodynamically stable and creates the barrier properties of the membrane.
questions.keyConceptTested: phospholipid bilayer — hydrophilic heads out; hydrophobic tails in

questions.stem: A chemotherapy drug inhibits topoisomerase II, an enzyme that relieves DNA strand tension during replication. Which cell cycle phase is most directly targeted?

options.A: G1 — cell growth and preparation
options.B: S phase — DNA replication
options.C: G2 — post-replication quality check
options.D: M phase — chromosome segregation

questions.rationale: Topoisomerase II is essential during S phase, when DNA is being replicated. As the double helix unwinds for copying, it creates torsional stress ahead of the replication fork. Topoisomerase II cuts and re-joins DNA strands to relieve this tension. Inhibiting it (e.g., with etoposide, doxorubicin) causes DNA breaks that halt replication and trigger apoptosis — targeting rapidly dividing cancer cells in S phase.
questions.keyConceptTested: topoisomerase II — S phase; chemotherapy mechanism
